Lamar (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Nelson Dingley (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Neuralia (British, 9182 GRT, built 1912), Norman Monarch (British, 7005 GRT, built 1943), Ocean Trader (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Peter V. Daniel (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), R.C. Brennan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Roald Amundsen (Norwegian, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Robert Treat Paine (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), Samythian (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Taria (Dutch (tanker), 10354 GRT, built 1939), Thomas B. Reed (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Thomas Lynch (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Thomas Sumter (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942) and Wiley Post (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). The naval oil barge (self propelled) USS Pumper also joined the convoy.
On 10 April 1944, the merchant vessel Cid was detached to Malta.

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Malta; Balteako (British, 1328 GRT, built 1920), Belgian Veteran (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Graiglas (British, 4312 GRT, built 1940), Sameveron (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944) and Samkey (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).

On 11 April 1944, the following merchant vessels were detached to Tunis / Bizerta; Empire Rosalind, Fort Brandon, Ima, Mark Twain, Ocean Trader, R.C. Brennan, Roald Amundsen and Thomas Lynch. USS Pumper was also detached to Bizerta.

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Tunis; Fylingdale (British, 3918 GRT, built 1924), George H. Thomas (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John M. Harlan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Maya (Honduran, 5528 GRT, built 1929) and William Kent (American, 7187 GRT, built 1942).

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Cyrus W. Field (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Darien (Panamanian, 3683 GRT, built 1924), Hugh Williamson (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Pallade (Italian, 1152 GRT, built 1899) and Paul H. Harwood (American (tanker), 7192 GRT, built 1918).

At 1245 Z/11 the British escort was detached to Bizerta [apparently HMS Myosotis proceeded to Malta] and a new escort joined. This escort was made up of the destroyers USS Decatur (Lt. D.G. Wright, USNR, with COMTASKFOR 64, Capt. H.S. Berdine, USCG on board), USS Whipple (T/Lt.Cdr. S.E. Woodard, USN, with COMDESDIV 57, T/Cdr. E.W. Yancey, USN, on board), USS Alden (Lt. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and the destroyer escorts USS Sellstrom (Cdr. W.L. Maloney, USCG, with COMCORTDIV 23, Cdr. F.P. Vetterick, USNR, on board), USS Ramsden (Lt.Cdr. S.T. Baketel, USCGR), USS Mills (Lt.Cdr. J.S. Muzzy, USCG), USS Rhodes (Cdr. E.A. Coffin, Jr., USCG), USS Richey (Cdr. P.DuP. Mills, USCG), USS Savage (Cdr. O.C. Rohnke, USCG), USS Tomich (Lt. C.B. Brown, USNR) and USS Sloat (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Cashman, USNR). As additional escort the AA cruiser HrMs Jacob van Heemskerck (Capt. W. Harmsen, RNethN) also joined the convoy.
At 0015 Z/12, USS Tomich fouled her rudder and screws with an obstruction and she was ordered to proceed to Oran to clear. She later rejoined at 1130 Z/14.
At 0500 Z/12, the following merchant vessels were detached to Bone; Belgian Airman, Belgian Veteran, Comedian, Empire Addison, Fort Michipicoten, Hardingham, Lyminge and Norman Monarch.

At the same time the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bone; Empire Trent (British, 5006 GRT, built 1927), Everleigh (British, 5222 GRT, built 1930), Janine (French, 2345 GRT, built 1918) and Novasli (Norwegian, 3204 GRT, built 1920).
At 1030 Z/12, the merchant vessel Barrgrove (British, 5222 GRT, built 1918) joined the convoy coming from Philippeville.
At the same time, the merchant vessel Janine was detached to Bougie.

At 1200 Z/13, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Atjutant (British, 1931 GRT, built 1922), Atlantic City (British, 5133 GRT, built 1941), Clan Cameron (British, 7243 GRT, built 1937), Clearpool (British, 5405 GRT, built 1935), Donacilla (British (tanker), 8113 GRT, built 1939), Edward N. Hurley (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Empire Shearwater (British, 4970 GRT, built 1920), Empire Symbol (British, 7027 GRT, built 1943), Fort Maurepas (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Joseph Alston (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Lesto (British, 1893 GRT, 1918), Morrison R. Waite (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Nyhorn (Norwegian, 4494 GRT, built 1929), Robert Jordan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Robert M.T. Hunter (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Samearn (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Taxiarchis (Greek, 4221 GRT, built 1913) and Walter E. Ranger (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). Also joining from Algiers was the submarine HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN).
At the same time the following merchant vessels were detached to Algiers; Askeladden, Corcrest, Egero, Everleigh, Fort Remy, Glenstrae, Neuralia, Samythian and Thomas Sumter.

At 1130 Z/14, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Oran; Benjamin R. Milam (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Christopher Gadsen (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Dilworth (American (tanker), 7045 GRT, built 1920), Ezra Cornell (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Fanad Head (British, 5038 GRT, built 1941), Felipe de Neve (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Fort Frederick (British, 7135 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pine (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Francis L. Lee (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), George B. McClellan (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Henry Durant (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Isaac Sharpless (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John B. Gordon (American, 7201 GRT, built 1943), M. M. Guhin (American, 7180 GRT, 1943), Mary Wilkins Freeman (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Niobe (Panamanian (tanker), 7299 GRT, built 1915), Samson (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Tanafjord (Norwegian, 5922 GRT, built 1921). The naval stores ship USS Polaris (T/Cdr. C.R. Miller, USN) also joined the convoy. Also joining were the destroyer USS Lansdale (T/Cdr. D.M. Swift, USN) and the minesweeper USS Pilot (Lt.Cdr. S.B. Wetmore, USNR), which had escorted the joiners from Oran, joined the convoy. Also USS Tomich rejoined the convoy.
At the same time the following merchant vessels were detached to Oran; Adjutant, Crosby S. Noyes, Empire Trent, Lesto and Taxiarchis.
At 2000 Z/14, USS John D. Edwards left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1108 Z/15.
At 0200 Z/15, USS Lansdale parted company with the convoy.
At 0240 Z/15, USS Whipple left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1255 Z/15.
At 0920 Z/15, USS Alden left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1800 Z/15.
At 1108 Z/15, USS John D. Edwards rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1255 Z/15, USS Whipple rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1315 Z/15, HrMs Jacob van Heemskerck left to convoy to proceed to Gibraltar.

At 1600 Z/15, the destroyer USS John D. Ford (T/Lt.Cdr. J.S. Slaughter, USN) joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar where she had been undergoing temporary repairs to repair damage sustained from a collision on 29 March.
At 1800 Z/15, USS Decatur left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 2045 Z/15. USS Alden had just rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1845 Z/15, HMS Uproar parted company to proceed to Gibraltar escorted by USS Mills.

At 1900 Z/15, the merchant vessel Joshua Henry (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar. She was escorted to the rendezvous by the patrol vessels USS PC-471 (Lt. G.B. Calkins, USNR) and USS PC-480 (Lt.(jg) J.K. Miller, USNR) which also joined the convoy.
At the same time the merchant vessels Amberton, Anna Knudsen, Balteako, Empire Shearwater, Fylingdale, Graiglas, Novasli and Pallade were detached to Gibraltar.
At 2045 Z/15, USS Decatur rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 2200 Z/15, rejoined the convoy after having escorted HMS Uproar to Gibraltar.

At 1200 Z/16, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Frederick Banting (American, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Lorraine (French (tanker), 9512 GRT, built 1937), Newton D. Baker (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Pan-Maryland (American (tanker), 7701 GRT, built 1938), Sambay (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samchess (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Samota (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samovar (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and William J. Bryan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). Also the naval tanker USS Kennebec (Cdr. M.D. Mullen, USNR) joined coming from Casablanca. They had been escorted to the rendezvous by the destroyers HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN), HMS Anthony (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Wallace, DSC, RN), HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. J. Smallwood, RN), HMS Vanoc (Lt.Cdr. P.R. Ward, RN) and the patrol vessel USS PC-472 (Lt.Cdr. P.A. Dallis, Jr., USNR). These escort vessels temporary joined the convoy.

At 1800 Z/16, the following merchant vessels parted company with the convoy to proceed to Casablanca; Barrgrove, Clearpool, Empire Symbol, Fort Cataraqui, Fort Frederick, Nyhorn, Robert M.T. Hunter, Samearn and Sameveron. USS PC-471, USS PC-472 and USS PC-480 went with them as escorts. The four British destroyers which had joined at 1200 Z/16 also parted company in accordance with their orders.

At 0700 Z/17, the merchant vessel Niobe straggled from the convoy. She was ordered to continue on the the USA via the stragglers route but later information was received that she was ordered to proceed to the Azores.
On 19 April 1944, USS Decatur and USS Whipple fuelled from USS Kennebec.
On 20 April 1944, USS John D. Ford, USS John D. Edwards, USS Alden and USS Pilot fuelled from USS Kennebec.
At 1045 Z/20, the merchant vessels Fort Anne (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943) and Hoke Smith (American, 7199 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from the Azores.
On 23 April 1944, USS Decatur fuelled from USS Kennebec.
On 27 April 1944, USS Decatur, USS Whipple, USS Alden, USS John D. Edwards and USS John D. Ford fuelled from USS Kennebec.

At 1300 Z/29, USS Kennebec parted company with the convoy to proceed to Bermuda. The destroyer escorts USS Edmonds (T/Cdr. C.S. Barker, Jr., USN) and USS Shelton (Lt.Cdr. L.G. Salomon, USNR) had just made rendezvous with the convoy to escort the tanker to her destination.

At 0001 Z/1, the Hapton Roads section of the convoy, made up of 38 merchant vessels parted company. USS Whipple, USS Alden, USS John D. Edwards, USS John D. Ford and USS Pilot went with them as escorts.
At 1100 Z/1, the Delaware Capes section of 5 merchant vessels was detached. USS Ramsden and USS Savage went with them as escorts.
At 2100 Z/1, two merchant vessels were detached to St. John, New Brunswick.
At 0250 Z/2, one merchant vessel was detached to Philadelphia. The remainder of the convoy entered New York harbour later that morning. (61)

9 Apr 1944 During the night of 9/10 April 1944, HMS Vampire (Lt. C.W. Taylor, RNR), conducted A/S exercises off Gibraltar with HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) and HMS Anthony (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Wallace, DSC, RN). (62)
This convoy departed Freetown on 30 July 1944.

On departure from Freetown the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bosphorus (Norwegian, 2111 GRT, built 1934), Bothnia (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Calgary (British, 7206 GRT, built 1921), Fauzon (French, 4376 GRT, built 1938), Fort Cadotte (British, 7128 GRT, built 1943), Fort de Troyon (French, 5131 GRT, built 1919), Fort Panmure (French, 7155 GRT, built 1943), Fort Paskoyac (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Greystoke Castle (British, 5853 GRT, built 1928), Henri Jaspar (Belgian, 5760 GRT, built 1929), Ittersum (Dutch, 5199 GRT, built 1938), Northleigh (British, 5450 GRT, built 1937), Norwegian (British, 6366 GRT, built 1921), Wearwood (British, 4597 GRT, built 1930), Yearby (British, 5666 GRT, built 1929) and Zypenberg (Dutch, 4973 GRT, built 1920).

On departure from Freetown the convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Enchantress (Lt.Cdr. E.D.J. Abbot, DSC, RN), frigate HMS Ness (T/A/Lt.Cdr. R.H. Marchington, MBE, RNVR) and the patrol vessel HMS Kilbirnie (T/Lt. E.R.H. Seddon, RNVR).

At 0800 Z/2, in position 14°44'N, 18°00'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Dakar; John Laurance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Marrakech (French, 6179 GRT, built 1914).
At 0945 Z/4, in position 21.14'N, 18.01'W, the John Laurance parted company to proceed to Port Etienne to due to one of the crew being seriously ill.
At 0900 Z/10, in position 35°15'N, 10°28'W, the Fauzan and Marrakech were detached to Casablanca.

Around 1030 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Cap Cantin (British (former French), 3317 GRT, built 1933), Fort Ellice (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942) and Porjus (Swedish, 2965 GRT, built 1906).

These ships were escorted from Casablanca to the rendezvous by the minesweeping sloop Gazelle (?) and the patrol vessel L'Effronté (?) which subsequently escorted the two merchant vessels that had parted company with the convoy to Casablanca where they arrived on the 11th.
At the same time as the Casablanca section joined up convoy MKS 57(G) merged with convoy SL 166 into the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G).

The following merchant vessels made up convoy MKS 57(G) which now thus joined; Baron Douglas (British, 3899 GRT, built 1932), City of Dundee (British, 5273 GRT, built 1921), Clan MacNeil (British, 6111 GRT, built 1922), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Edward Bruce (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Empire Torrent (British, 7076 GRT, built 1942), Fort Prudhomme (British, 7167 GRT, built 1943), Manchester Division (British, 6048 GRT, built 1918), Marpessa (Dutch (tanker, serving as escort oiler), 7408 GRT, built 1927), Marsdale (British, 4890 GRT, built 1940), Nurani (British, 5414 GRT, built 1941), Ocean Pelgrim (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Pierre Soule (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Sarpedon (British, 11321 GRT, built 1923) and Sheaf Crown (British, 4868 GRT, built 1929). With these merchant vessels the rescue ship Goodwin (British, 1570 GRT, built 1917) and the submarines HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) also joined the convoy.
The escort of convoy KMS 57(G) took over the escort of the now combined convoy and HMS Enchantress and HMS Ness proceeded to Gibraltar arriving there on the 11th.

The new escort was made up of the frigates HMS Helmsdale (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Foley (A/Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR) and the corvettes HMS Kenilworth Castle (Lt. J.J.Allon, RNR), HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR), HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Portchester Castle (Lt. A.G. Scott, RNR).
At the time the convoys merged the destroyer HMS Highlander (Cdr. D.A. Rayner, DSC, VRD, RNVR) also joined the convoy escort.

At 1050 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the Norwegian was detached by the Commodore to proceed to Casablanca due to a defective boiler. HMS Kilbirnie was given the task to escort her there. They arrived at Casablanca on the 11th.
At 1000 Z/11, in position 36°00'N, 14°18'W, the escort carrier HMS Campania (A/Capt. K.A. Short, RN) joined the combined convoy coming from the combined convoy OS 85 / KMS 59.
At 1600 Z/11, in position 36°17'N, 15°23'W, the frigate HMS Bayntun (Lt.Cdr. L.P. Bourke, RNZNR) joined the convoy.
At 1400 Z/12, in position 38°38'N, 18°00'W, the destroyer HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) joined the convoy.
At 2000 Z/15, in position 48°52'N, 18°20'W, the Marpesa parted company with the convoy to join convoy OS 86.

At 0730 Z/16, in position 50°20'N, 17°54'W, the following merchant vessels of the convoy parted company to proceed ahead to the U.K. as fast section; Bosphorus, Greystoke Castle, Henri Jaspar and Marsdale. They took HMS Campania, HMS Antelope, HMS Foley and HMS HMS Portchester Castle with them as escorts. The fast section was dispersed at 0230 B/18 in the North Channel.
At 0700 B/18, HMS Helmsdale, HMS Kenilworth Castle, HMS Launceston Castle and HMS Pevensey Castle were detached to Londonderry where they arrived later the same day.
At 0740 B/18, the following merchant vessels were detached to proceed towards the North Channel. This were the Clyde, Mersey, Belfast and Bristol Channel sections of the convoy.
The Clyde section was made up of the following merchant vessels; City of Dundee, Cydonia, Pierre Soule and Zypenberg. The rescue ship Goodwin also went to the Clyde with them.
The Mersey section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bothnia, Calgary, Clan MacNeil, Edward Bruce, Empire Torrent, Fort Cadotte, Fort Panmure and Ittersum.
The Belfast section was made up of only one merchant vessel, the Porjus.
The Bristol Channel section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Fort Ellice and Ocean Pilgrim.

HMS Unruly and HMS Unsparing also parted company to proceed with the North Channel section. At 1615 B/18, they parted company with the North Channel section to proceed to Dundee escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Lt. H.J. Bartlett, DSC, RN).
The North Channel section was escorted by HMS Highlander which arrived at Londonderry on the 19th.

At 1200 B/19, the following merchant vessels arrived at Loch Ewe for onward routing to east coast ports; Baron Douglas, Cap Cantin, Fort de Troyon, Fort Paskoyac, Fort Prudhomme, Manchester Division, Northleigh, Nurani, Sarpedon, Sheaf Crown, Wearwood and Yearby. They were escorted to Loch Ewe by HMS Bayntun which subsequently proceeded to Londonderry where she arrived later on 19 August. (63)
